source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.6 Securing (pp. 116-118)

questions.q: Permanently lock the account of the user albert using passwd.
questions.why: passwd -l locks the account by prefixing the shadow password field (adds !!); -u unlocks it.

options: The status of the account (locked, password set, ageing, ...)
questions.why: passwd -S reports the account status; like -l/-u/-d it is for root (uid=0) use.

questions.q: As root, change the password of the user alain.
questions.why: root can set any user's password with 'passwd <login>'; a normal user runs 'passwd' alone to change their own (after giving the old one).

questions.q: In a script, what does 'echo "azerty,1" | passwd --stdin philippe' do?
options: Sets philippe's password to the piped value (passwd encrypts it); the password is passed in clear on the pipe
questions.why: --stdin reads the new password from standard input, handy for scripted account setup; passwd encrypts it, but it is supplied in clear text.

questions.q: Display the password-ageing policy details for the user alain using chage.
questions.why: chage -l lists the account's ageing policy (last change, expiry, min/max, warning).

questions.q: For alain, set the minimum password age to 60 days, maximum to 90, warning to 80 and inactivity delay to 10, using chage.
questions.why: chage -m minimum, -M maximum, -W warning days, -I inactivity delay after expiry.

questions.q: Force the user philippe to change their password at next login (set last-change to 0) using chage.
questions.why: chage -d 0 resets the last-change date to the epoch, so the password is considered expired and must be changed on next login.

questions.q: Which chage option sets the ACCOUNT expiration date (as YYYY-MM-DD or a day timestamp)?
questions.why: chage -E sets the account expiry date; -M sets the maximum PASSWORD lifetime, which is a different thing.
